**Newcastle International Sports Centre**, currently known as **McDonald Jones Stadium** for sponsorship reasons, is a multi-purpose sports [stadium](/wiki/Stadium "Stadium") located in [Newcastle](/wiki/Newcastle,_New_South_Wales "Newcastle, New South Wales"), Australia. The ground is home to the [Newcastle Knights](/wiki/Newcastle_Knights "Newcastle Knights") ([National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League")) and [Newcastle Jets FC](/wiki/Newcastle_Jets_FC "Newcastle Jets FC") ([A-League](/wiki/A-League "A-League")). It is owned by the [Government of New South Wales](/wiki/Government_of_New_South_Wales "Government of New South Wales") and is administered by [Venues NSW](/wiki/Venues_NSW "Venues NSW"). Due to past sponsorship deals, the ground has been previously known as **Marathon Stadium**, **[EnergyAustralia](/wiki/EnergyAustralia "EnergyAustralia") Stadium**, **[Ausgrid](/wiki/Ausgrid "Ausgrid") Stadium** and **Hunter Stadium**. Newcastle International Sports Centre is also known as **Newcastle Stadium** when in use during [AFC](/wiki/Asian_Football_Confederation "Asian Football Confederation") competitions due to conflicting sponsorship reasons.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newcastle_International_Sports_Centre&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Work began on the stadium on 1 December 1967, and was officially opened by [Queen Elizabeth II](/wiki/Elizabeth_II "Elizabeth II") on 10 April 1970. It was originally known as the *International Sports Centre*, and is still part of the ISC complex to this day. The playing surface back then was originally oval shaped to allow both codes of rugby, soccer and cricket to be able to make use of it.

The [Newcastle Knights](/wiki/Newcastle_Knights "Newcastle Knights") secured a lease on the stadium in 1986, and converted it from an oval to a rectangle layout. During the [1988 Great Britain Lions tour](/wiki/1988_Great_Britain_Lions_tour "1988 Great Britain Lions tour"), the Newcastle Knights, in their first season, hosted a match at the ground. On that occasion the Lions, captained by [Ellery Hanley](/wiki/Ellery_Hanley "Ellery Hanley"), defeated the Knights 28–12.

In 1992, local tyre outlet Marathon Tyres became the naming rights sponsor for the stadium, and it was renamed *Marathon Stadium*. That year the Knights played [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain_Lions "Great Britain Lions") for a second time as part of the Lions [Tour of Australasia](/wiki/1992_Great_Britain_Lions_tour_of_Australasia "1992 Great Britain Lions tour of Australasia"). The Ellery Hanley captained Lions took the Knights apart winning 22–0. Later in the [1992 NSWRL season](/wiki/1992_NSWRL_season "1992 NSWRL season"), the Knights qualified for their first ever Finals series.

Towards the end of 2001, energy supplier [EnergyAustralia](/wiki/EnergyAustralia "EnergyAustralia") took over naming rights, and thus the stadium became *EnergyAustralia Stadium*. In February 2011 it was announced that the stadium would be renamed Ausgrid Stadium after EnergyAustralia was renamed [Ausgrid](/wiki/Ausgrid "Ausgrid").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Before redevelopment, the stadium had a capacity of 28,000, including 5,000 in the main grandstand. The ground attendance record for a sporting event at the venue prior to the redevelopments is 32,642, which was set when the Knights took on the [Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles](/wiki/Manly-Warringah_Sea_Eagles "Manly-Warringah Sea Eagles") in July 1995.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Following the retirement of former Knights captain [Andrew Johns](/wiki/Andrew_Johns "Andrew Johns"), the new eastern grandstand was renamed *The Andrew Johns Stand* in honour of Johns in front of a packed crowd before the Knights vs [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") NRL match on 22 April 2007.

The name (Newcastle International Sports Centre) is used primarily by those who wish to mention stadiums by original names, such as non-commercial organisations like the [Australian Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Australian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Australian Broadcasting Corporation"), and those with other corporate interests such as [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") or the [Asian Football Confederation](/wiki/Asian_Football_Confederation "Asian Football Confederation").

On 16 October 2011, the venue's all-time record sporting attendance was set with 32,890 attending the [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_rugby_league_team "Australia men's national rugby league team") vs [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_national_rugby_league_team "New Zealand national rugby league team") Rugby League test match.

In October 2016, McDonald Jones Homes, a local Newcastle company, was named as the new naming rights sponsor of the stadium. This new deal left behind a five year gap between naming rights sponsors at the ground.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Redevelopments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newcastle_International_Sports_Centre&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Redevelopments")]

#### 2003–05

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newcastle_International_Sports_Centre&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2003–05")]

The stadium underwent redevelopment during the years 2003–05, funded mostly by local and state government grants.

Factors that brought on the redevelopment included:

* non-compliance of [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") (NRL) stadium criteria, especially the dressing rooms;
* failure to attract major sporting events to the area, specifically the [2003 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/2003_Rugby_World_Cup "2003 Rugby World Cup");
* inadequate and ageing spectator and corporate facilities;
* covered [seating capacity](/wiki/Seating_capacity "Seating capacity") well below best practice and NRL standards;
* minimal areas within the grandstand to increase members' patronage both during a sporting event and on non-match-days;
* poor facilities for media officials;
* unsatisfactory temporary spectator facilities to the north and south of the western stand;
* the perceived need for the incumbent [State](/wiki/Government_of_New_South_Wales "Government of New South Wales") [Labor](/wiki/Australian_Labor_Party_(New_South_Wales_Branch) "Australian Labor Party (New South Wales Branch)") government to contribute to the [public infrastructure](/wiki/Public_works "Public works") in a [region](/wiki/Hunter_Region "Hunter Region") of [safe seats](/wiki/Safe_seat "Safe seat").[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The first stage of the redevelopment was completed in early 2004. This consisted of;

* The construction of the lower level of the Eastern Stand (brought into operation for the 2004 NRL season and Anzac Test between Australia and New Zealand). This level holds 7,700 people.
* The relocation of corporate boxes and seating to the North and South stands.

The second stage of construction began in 2004 and was completed in 2005. This consisted of:

* The construction of the Eastern Stand's second level of seating and corporate boxes;
* a new video screen; and
* remedial work for the Western Grandstand, including updating the media facilities.

#### 2008–11

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newcastle_International_Sports_Centre&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2008–11")]

During the [2007 NSW Election](/wiki/2007_New_South_Wales_state_election "2007 New South Wales state election") campaign, the [Premier](/wiki/Premiers_of_New_South_Wales "Premiers of New South Wales") [Morris Iemma](/wiki/Morris_Iemma "Morris Iemma") promised [$](/wiki/Australian_dollar "Australian dollar")30 million towards an upgrade of the stadium, conditional on the [Federal Government](/wiki/Parliament_of_Australia "Parliament of Australia") matching the funds.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

On 1 April 2008 the federal government confirmed $10m towards the development of the Western Grandstand. This was in addition to the $30m commitment from the state government. This was a critical step for the stadium's development for the upgrade to be in by 2011.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The [A$](/wiki/Australian_dollar "Australian dollar")40 million contributed to an expansion of the stadium's capacity to eventually hold over 40,000 as well as general improvements in the stadiums facilities.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

From 2008 to 2010 the stadium was upgraded again[[13]](#cite_note-theherald.yourguide.com.au-13) to around 33 000 seats, with a hope for the stadium to be involved in the [2015 Asian Cup](/wiki/AFC_Asian_Cup "AFC Asian Cup") and [2018 World Cup](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup_bids "2018 FIFA World Cup bids") were Australia be the host of those tournaments (Australia did host the Asian Cup, but not the World Cup). As part of the announcement, Morris Iemma stated that the capacity of 33,000 could be increased to the 40,000 necessary for World Cup Hosting, through temporary seating.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The total cost of the upgrade was estimated as $60 million, with $50 million from the state government and $10 million from the Federal Government.
Although construction was slated to take place during both the [Newcastle Knights](/wiki/Newcastle_Knights "Newcastle Knights") and [Newcastle Jets](/wiki/Newcastle_Jets "Newcastle Jets") seasons, developers stated that there would be minimal effect on attendances due to the staged approach.[[13]](#cite_note-theherald.yourguide.com.au-13)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/Newcastle_Stadium_at_night_from_the_south_end.jpg/250px-Newcastle_Stadium_at_night_from_the_south_end.jpg)](/wiki/File:Newcastle_Stadium_at_night_from_the_south_end.jpg)

McDonald Jones Stadium at night

The stadium development was planned to be carried out over four stages.

* Stage 1 (now completed) included dressing, warm up and medical rooms, with showers, toilets, ice baths and 855-seats of the spectator concourse in the stadium's south-west.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
* Stage 2 (now completed) replicates Stage 1 on the northern side of the Western Grandstand and also included the main western stand's lower concourse.
* Stage 3 (now completed) demolished the old western Grandstand and is replacing it with one similar to the eastern stand.
* Stage 4 (undertaking preparation) will involve building the northern and southern ends of the ground, replacing the grass hills with seated areas.

#### Newcastle Knights Centre of Excellence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newcastle_International_Sports_Centre&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Newcastle Knights Centre of Excellence")]

In June 2018, plans were announced for a Centre of Excellence for the Newcastle Knights located at the southern end of the ground at a cost of $20 million with $10 million provided by the NSW government with the other $10 million funded by the Knights' owners The Wests Group.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

#### 2024 upgrades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newcastle_International_Sports_Centre&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2024 upgrades")]

Prior to the start of the [2024 NRL season](/wiki/2024_NRL_season "2024 NRL season"), the two video screens at the ground were replaced with two 160sqm screens along with a series of minor upgrades to spectator facilities.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newcastle_International_Sports_Centre&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Transport")]

The nearest railway station is [Broadmeadow](/wiki/Broadmeadow_railway_station "Broadmeadow railway station"). [Newcastle Transport](/wiki/Newcastle_Transport "Newcastle Transport") operate a shuttle service from [Queens Wharf](/wiki/Queens_Wharf "Queens Wharf") on match days.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

## Uses

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Hunter_Stadium_Day.JPG/250px-Hunter_Stadium_Day.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hunter_Stadium_Day.JPG)

Hunter Stadium during the [2015 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2015_AFC_Asian_Cup "2015 AFC Asian Cup") semi-final between [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_soccer_team "Australia men's national soccer team") and the [UAE](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates_national_football_team "United Arab Emirates national football team").

### Rugby league

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newcastle_International_Sports_Centre&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Rugby league")]

The [Newcastle Knights](/wiki/Newcastle_Knights "Newcastle Knights") of the [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") team have been tenants of the ground since their formation in 1988.

In October 2011 the ground hosted a [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") one-off test match between Australia and New Zealand. This event set a new ground attendance record for a sporting event of 32,890.

### Soccer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newcastle_International_Sports_Centre&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Soccer")]

During the [National Soccer League](/wiki/National_Soccer_League "National Soccer League") competition, three clubs have played their home ground games at this stadium. They are [Newcastle KB United](/wiki/Newcastle_KB_United "Newcastle KB United") (1978–84); [Newcastle Rosebud United](/wiki/Adamstown_Rosebud_FC "Adamstown Rosebud FC") (1984–86) and [Newcastle United](/wiki/Newcastle_Jets_FC "Newcastle Jets FC") (2000–04). The NSL competition folded in 2004.

In 2005, the newly formed national competition ([A-League](/wiki/A-League "A-League")) began to operate. [Newcastle United Jets](/wiki/Newcastle_United_Jets "Newcastle United Jets") was part of the newly formed competition, and have played at this ground ever since.

The stadium also hosted two [2015 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2015_AFC_Asian_Cup "2015 AFC Asian Cup") group stage matches, and also a semi final between [Australia](/wiki/Australia_men%27s_national_soccer_team "Australia men's national soccer team") and the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates_national_football_team "United Arab Emirates national football team") and a third place playoff between United Arab Emirates and [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq_national_football_team "Iraq national football team").

In February 2017 it was announced that the [National Premier Leagues Northern NSW](/wiki/National_Premier_Leagues_Northern_NSW "National Premier Leagues Northern NSW") Grand Final would be taking place at the ground in September that year. In the Grand Final between the [Edgeworth Eagles](/wiki/Edgeworth_Eagles "Edgeworth Eagles") and [Lambton Jaffas](/wiki/Lambton_Jaffas "Lambton Jaffas") a crowd of 4174 was on hand to see the Jaffas clinch the title beating the Eagles 2–0 deep into Extra Time.

The [2018 A-League Grand Final](/wiki/2018_A-League_Grand_Final "2018 A-League Grand Final") was held at the venue, contested by the Newcastle Jets and Melbourne Victory, on 5 May 2018.

### Baseball

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newcastle_International_Sports_Centre&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Baseball")]

The [Hunter Eagles](/wiki/Hunter_Eagles "Hunter Eagles") were formed for the [1994–95 Australian Baseball League season](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_Australian_Baseball_League_season "1994–95 Australian Baseball League season") after purchasing the [Sydney Wave](/wiki/Sydney_Wave "Sydney Wave")'s licence. The Eagles played in the Australian Baseball League until the end of the [1997–98](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_Australian_Baseball_League_season "1997–98 Australian Baseball League season") season.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Rugby union

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Newcastle_International_Sports_Centre&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Rugby union")]

In 2012, it was used for the first match of the [2012 Scotland rugby union tour of Oceania](/wiki/2012_Scotland_rugby_union_tour_of_Australia,_Fiji_and_Samoa "2012 Scotland rugby union tour of Australia, Fiji and Samoa"). Scotland won the match, beating Australia 9–6.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

The [New South Wales Waratahs](/wiki/New_South_Wales_Waratahs "New South Wales Waratahs") played a home fixture of their [Super Rugby](/wiki/Super_Rugby "Super Rugby") season from 2019 in Newcastle whilst the [Sydney Football Stadium](/wiki/Sydney_Football_Stadium_(2022) "Sydney Football Stadium (2022)") was rebuilt.[[22]](#cite_note-22)
